title: Cheap counterfactual strategy-gap features from lap-wise simulation
summary: Motorsport strategy papers explicitly model pit timing as a trade-off between tyre degradation benefit and pit-loss cost, and Monte Carlo race simulation is used to compare early vs late stops.
rationale: Motorsport strategy papers explicitly model pit timing as a trade-off between tyre degradation benefit and pit-loss cost, and Monte Carlo race simulation is used to compare early vs late stops. Even a crude per-row approximation of 'benefit if I stop now versus one lap later' gives the classifier a decision-theoretic feature instead of more raw ratios.
implementation_hint: Estimate per-race pit-loss proxy and per-compound local degradation slope, then compute features such as `delta_if_stop_now_vs_next_lap`, `gap_to_optimal_stint_length`, `expected_position_gain_if_cover_wave`, and robustness under reduced pit-loss scenarios. Start deterministic; optionally add a few Monte Carlo draws for safety-car-reduced pit loss and aggregate mean/std as features.
expected_effect: Injects first-order race-strategy economics that trees can combine with current lap context; likely most useful on borderline rows where tyre age alone is ambiguous.
risk: Misspecified pit-loss or degradation slope can add noise; must remain strictly causal and avoid using race-outcome information.
